title: Lemon Granita
subtitle: Frozen lemon syrup scraped into loose, crunchy crystals
excerpt: A Sicilian water ice made by freezing a sweetened lemon syrup and scraping it with a fork every 30 minutes until it forms an icy, grainy mass of pale yellow crystals. No machine required. The texture is coarser and crunchier than a sorbet, which is the point.
sourceNotes: Granita di limone is a Sicilian speciality associated with the eastern cities of Catania and Messina, where it is eaten for breakfast with a brioche, or as a mid-morning or afternoon refreshment. The technique of freezing a flavoured syrup and raking it with a fork predates the machine-churned sorbet and requires no equipment beyond a freezer and a fork.

content.text: Granita is the opposite of sorbet: where sorbet is churned to be as smooth as possible, granita is scraped repeatedly during freezing to create distinct, loose ice crystals. The coarse, crunchy texture is deliberate and correct. A granita that is smooth has been over-processed or incorrectly made. The technique is to freeze for 45 minutes, scrape with a fork, return to the freezer, and repeat until the whole tray is a mass of fluffy, crystalline flakes.
Use a wide, shallow metal tray rather than a deep one. The shallower the layer, the faster it freezes and the more evenly. Shallow trays also make the crystals easier to scrape.

items.name: Lemon
items.prepNote: finely grated zest of 2 and juice of all 4 (about 130 ml juice)

content.text: You will also need 350 ml of cold water. A wide, shallow metal or plastic tray, about 30 × 20 cm.

Combine {{caster-sugar}} of caster sugar with 350 ml of cold water in a saucepan. Heat over medium heat, stirring, until the sugar dissolves. Remove from the heat. Stir in the lemon zest and leave to infuse for 10 minutes, then strain. Stir in the juice of {{lemon}}. Leave to cool completely.

Pour the cooled mixture into a wide, shallow metal tray. Freeze for 45 minutes until ice crystals have formed around the edges. Scrape with a fork, breaking up the ice and mixing the frozen edges into the liquid centre. Return to the freezer. Repeat every 30 minutes for 2.5, 3 hours until the entire tray is a loose, crystalline mass with no liquid remaining. Serve scooped into chilled glasses.

items.fix: Leave at room temperature for 15, 20 minutes until the edges soften, then scrape again vigorously with a fork. Repeat the freezing and scraping cycle to restore the texture.
items.cause: Missed the scraping window and left it too long

items.fix: The ratio here is correct. Do not add more sugar. Return the mixture to the freezer without scraping and give it another 30 minutes, slushier mixtures take longer to crystallise.
items.cause: Too much sugar in the mixture
items.symptom: Granita is slushy and won't crystallise

items.fix: Zest only the yellow outer layer. Strain the juice through a sieve after squeezing to remove any pith fragments.
items.cause: Lemon pith was included in the zest, or pith was pressed through when juicing

items.fix: Use a shallow tray with a maximum depth of 2 cm. Shallow layers freeze faster and give more distinct crystals.
items.cause: Tray was too deep so the mixture froze slowly and the crystals merged
items.symptom: Very little texture difference between the crystals

content.text: Lemon granita is a Sicilian fixture. In the eastern cities of Catania and Messina people eat it for breakfast with a soft brioche. It is a morning ritual, not a dessert, mostly in summer. The granita is older than the machine-churned sorbet. The method is freezing a sweetened liquid in shallow trays and raking it at intervals for a coarse, icy texture. The lemon version is the most classic, though almond, coffee, and jasmine are also common in Sicily. The dish has spread wherever Sicilian food culture has gone.
